title: kernel-utils: hacking on Linux kernels from a Mac
text:
When you do kernel CTF or exploit-dev work, most of your time is spent on the same handful of chores: get a kernel building in a clean environment, boot it under QEMU with the right hardening flags, and wire up a debugger and a way to push your exploit into the guest. Doing this on a Mac adds friction, since the kernel build really wants a Linux toolchain.
kernel-utils is a small toolkit I put together to make that loop fast and repeatable from a macOS host. It installs three commands on your $PATH.

title: kernel-utils: hacking on Linux kernels from a Mac
text:
The three commands

kdev [path] — builds and drops you into a kernel-build Docker container, mounting your current directory (or path) at /work. This is where the Linux toolchain lives, so the actual make happens here.
krun [bzImage] [initramfs] — boots a kernel under QEMU with hardened, CTF-friendly settings: SMEP/SMAP/KPTI on, a gdbstub listening on :1234, and guest port 8000 forwarded to host 8080. It auto-discovers arch/x86/boot/bzImage, ./bzImage, and ./initramfs.cpio.gz in the current directory, so usually you just type krun.
kbuild-initramfs — builds an initramfs.cpio.gz in the current directory, copying files from your cwd into /exploit inside the image.

title: kernel-utils: hacking on Linux kernels from a Mac
text:
A typical session
mkdir ~/kernel-work && cd ~/kernel-work
kdev                                   # enter container, $PWD mounted at /work
# inside the container:
git clone --depth 1 -b v6.17 \
  git://git.kernel.org/pub/scm/linux/kernel/git/stable/linux.git
cd linux
make defconfig
make -j$(nproc) bzImage
krun                                   # boots arch/x86/boot/bzImage
Once the guest is up, pulling in your exploit is a one-liner (the host’s QEMU forward is reachable from inside the guest at 10.0.2.2:8000):
rm exploit; wget http://10.0.2.2:8000/exploit && chmod +x ./exploit

title: kernel-utils: hacking on Linux kernels from a Mac
text:
Knobs worth knowing
A couple of environment variables tune krun:

KU_NIC — the QEMU NIC model. e1000 (default) or virtio (alias for virtio-net-pci). Only pick virtio if your kernel has CONFIG_VIRTIO_NET=y and CONFIG_VIRTIO_PCI=y, otherwise eth0 won’t show up in the guest.
KASLR — 0 (default) appends nokaslr to the kernel cmdline, which is the CTF-friendly default. Set KASLR=1 to enable it.

KU_NIC=virtio KASLR=1 krun

title: kernel-utils: hacking on Linux kernels from a Mac
text:
Install
pipx install .          # or: pip install -e .
That’s the whole thing — three commands that turn “I want to debug a kernel bug” into about a minute of setup instead of an afternoon of yak-shaving.
